name: Abbey
description: A classic cocktail and the first drink in The Savoy Cocktail Book from 1930. Still well known in Britain, it blends gin, Lillet Blanc, orange juice, and a dash of orange bitters.
ingredients:
Gin – 45 ml
 Lillet Blanc – 20 ml
 Orange Juice – 20 ml
 Orange Bitters – 2 dash
 Cherry – 1

name: Adam & Eve
description: This variation on the Old Fashioned gets a pleasant herbal kick from Galliano liqueur, adding vanilla and anise notes to the bourbon base with simple syrup and Angostura bitters.
ingredients:
Bourbon – 60 ml
 Galliano – 15 ml
 Simple Syrup – 7 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 3 dash

name: Adonis
description: The Adonis cocktail was most likely named after a play (the first Broadway play to run more than 500 shows) in the 19th century, which was also a time when sherry was extremely popular.
ingredients:
Dry Sherry – 45 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 20 ml
 Orange Bitters – 1 dash
 Orange Peel – 1

name: Airmail
description: A rum-based champagne cocktail likely created in 1930s Cuba, possibly by Bacardi to celebrate the launch of airmail service. Published in Esquire's 1949 Handbook for Hosts.
ingredients:
Rum – 60 ml
 Lime Juice – 15 ml
 Honey – 5.0 ml
 Sparkling Wine – 150 ml

name: Alabama Slammer
description: Also sometimes known as a Southern Slammer, this fruity drink was popular in the 1980s and was often available in pitcher size at chain restaurants. It blends vodka with amaretto and Southern Comfort.
ingredients:
Vodka – 45 ml
 Orange Juice – 30 ml
 Amaretto – 15 ml
 Southern Comfort – 15 ml
 Grenadine – 7 ml
 Orange – 1 slice

name: Alaska
description: A variation on the martini replacing dry vermouth with yellow Chartreuse, a liqueur produced by Carthusian monks in France. A classic cocktail dating to the early 20th century.
ingredients:
Gin – 60 ml
 Yellow Chartreuse – 15 ml

name: Alfonso
description: Named after Spanish King Alfonso XIII, who is said to have tried this cocktail while in France. A sugar cube soaked in Angostura bitters is topped with Dubonnet and sparkling wine.
ingredients:
Sugar Cube – 1
 Angostura Bitters – 4 dash
 Dubonnet – 15 ml
 Sparkling Wine – 120 ml

name: Algonquin
description: Named for the famed Algonquin Hotel in New York City. Since the hotel didn't serve alcohol until after Prohibition, this rye, vermouth, and pineapple cocktail likely dates to the mid-1930s.
ingredients:
Rye – 60 ml
 Dry Vermouth – 30 ml
 Pineapple Juice – 30 ml
 Cherry – 1

name: Amaretto Sour
description: A smooth, sweet cocktail ideal for those who prefer an easy-drinking drink. The almond-flavored amaretto is balanced by fresh lemon juice for a simple yet satisfying sour.
ingredients:
Amaretto – 60 ml
 Lemon Juice – 30 ml
 Lemon – 1 wedge
 Cherry – 1

name: American Variation
description: Based on the American Trilogy cocktail from Little Branch in New York City. A nice combination of rye whiskey, Grand Marnier, apple brandy, and both Angostura and Peychaud's bitters.
ingredients:
Rye – 60 ml
 Grand Marnier – 15 ml
 Apple Brandy – 15 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 1 dash
 Peychaud's Bitters – 1 dash
 Lemon Peel – 1 twist

name: Americana
description: A very pleasant sparkling cocktail where smooth Tennessee whiskey meets champagne. A sugar cube soaked in Angostura bitters adds depth to this elegant, celebratory drink.
ingredients:
Sugar Cube – 1
 Angostura Bitters – 4 dash
 Tennessee Whiskey – 30 ml
 Sparkling Wine – 150 ml

name: Americano
description: Originally dubbed the Americano because of its popularity with US tourists in Italy during Prohibition. The recipe was created at Gaspare's Campari Bar in Turin in 1861.
ingredients:
Campari – 45 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 45 ml
 Club Soda – 90 ml
 Orange Peel – 1

name: Angostura Fizz
description: Also called a Trinidad Fizz, this cocktail comes from Charles Baker's 1939 "Gentleman's Companion." Unusual for a cocktail, this drink uses Angostura bitters as the base spirit.
ingredients:
Egg white – 15 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 30 ml
 Lime Juice – 30 ml
 Simple Syrup – 7 ml
 Grenadine – 7 ml
 Cream – 15 ml
 Club Soda – 30 ml

name: Atlas
description: A rich, spicy after-dinner cocktail first published in the late 1930s. Dark rum meets triple sec and Calvados for a spirit-forward blend of fruit and woody notes.
ingredients:
Dark Rum – 45 ml
 Triple Sec – 30 ml
 Calvados – 15 ml
 Orange – 1 slice

name: Barlovento
description: An easy, refreshing tropical drink served at the Barlovento Maloka hotel in Colombia's Tayrona Park. Light rum, pineapple, and orange juice get a surprising kick from a dash of hot sauce.
ingredients:
Light Rum – 60 ml
 Pineapple Juice – 45 ml
 Orange Juice – 45 ml
 Hot Sauce – 1 dash

name: Bee’s Knees
description: A Prohibition-era cocktail whose exact creator is unknown, though some attribute it to Frank Meier, a famous bartender at the Ritz in Paris in the 1920s. It blends gin, lemon, and honey.
ingredients:
Gin – 50 ml
 Lemon Juice – 25 ml
 Honey – 10 ml

name: Belle Amande
description: Sweet, fresh, and indulgent. Belle De Brillet pear liqueur and orgeat syrup create a perfect pear-almond harmony, brightened by lemon juice and lengthened with sparkling water.
ingredients:
Belle De Brillet – 50 ml
 Lemon Juice – 30 ml
 Orgeat Syrup – 15 ml
 Sparkling Water – 100 ml

name: Belle Bulle
description: All the freshness of the pear in a simple, refreshing drink. The lemon juice tempers the sweetness of the Belle De Brillet liqueur, topped with sparkling water for a light finish.
ingredients:
Belle De Brillet – 50 ml
 Lemon Juice – 20 ml
 Sparkling Water – 100 ml

name: Belle IPA
description: Freshness and simplicity in a glass. The sweet bitterness of IPA beer marries beautifully with the pear fruitiness of Belle De Brillet for an easy, refreshing two-ingredient beer cocktail.
ingredients:
Belle de Brillet – 40 ml 
 IPA Beer – 200 ml

name: Belle Mimosa
description: A variation of the classic Mimosa, the star cocktail of brunches sublimated by the pear! Belle De Brillet replaces the traditional triple sec, adding fruity depth to orange juice and champagne.
ingredients:
Belle de Brillet – 40 ml 
 Orange juice – 50 ml 
 Champagne – 120 ml

name: Belle Rosé
description: A fresh and fruity summer aperitif blending Belle De Brillet pear liqueur with rosé wine and tonic. The pear's sweetness complements the dry rosé, while tonic adds a crisp effervescence.
ingredients:
Belle de Brillet – 30 ml 
 Rosé wine – 80 ml 
 Tonic – 100 ml

name: Belle Summer
description: An original, fresh, and zesty summer cocktail with a touch of pear! Belle De Brillet is paired with dry vermouth and sparkling water for a light, effervescent warm-weather sipper.
ingredients:
Belle De Brillet – 40 ml
 Dry Vermouth – 20 ml
 Sparkling Water – 120 ml

name: Belle Tampico
description: A long, bittersweet, and refreshing drink. Belle De Brillet pear liqueur meets Campari's bitter edge, brightened by lemon juice and lengthened with tonic water for a vibrant, citrusy aperitif.
ingredients:
Belle de Brillet – 40 ml 
 Campari – 20 ml 
 Lemon juice – 15 ml 
 Tonic Water – 100 ml

name: Bennett
description: A variation on the gin sour family. Replace the lemon in a gin sour with lime and you get a gimlet; add Angostura bitters to a gimlet and you get the Bennett. Simple and elegant.
ingredients:
Gin – 60 ml
 Lime Juice – 30 ml
 Simple Syrup – 15 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 2 dash

name: Bentley
description: A deceptively simple cocktail from The Savoy Cocktail Book. Equal parts Calvados and Dubonnet combine to create a rich, fruity, and aromatic aperitif that belies its two-ingredient simplicity.
ingredients:
Calvados – 45 ml
 Dubonnet – 45 ml

name: Betsy Ross
description: Named after the legendary maker of the American flag, this version comes from David Embury's The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks. It combines brandy, ruby port, and a touch of orange curaçao.
ingredients:
Brandy – 45 ml
 Ruby Port – 15 ml
 Orange Curacao – 7 ml
 Lemon Peel – 1 twist

name: Between The Sheets
description: A Prohibition-era cocktail and the grandfather of all the suggestively named drinks that followed. Similar to a Sidecar, but with the addition of aged rum alongside the brandy.
ingredients:
Aged Rum – 30 ml
 Brandy – 30 ml
 Triple Sec – 20 ml
 Lime Juice – 15 ml

name: Bijou
description: One of the oldest cocktails in Harry Johnson's 1900 "New and Improved Bartender Manual." Bijou is French for jewel, and the drink blends gin, sweet vermouth, and green Chartreuse.
ingredients:
Gin – 20 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 20 ml
 Green Chartreuse – 20 ml
 Orange Bitters – 1 dash
 Orange Peel – 1
 Cherry – 1

name: Bitter Mai Tai
description: Created by Jeremy Ortel of Dram in New York. While you can taste the Mai Tai, the addition of Campari adds a bitter complexity. The mint garnish provides a fresh scent to the citrus aroma.
ingredients:
Orange Curacao – 15 ml
 Orgeat Syrup – 20 ml
 Dark Rum – 20 ml
 Lime Juice – 30 ml
 Campari – 45 ml
 Mint – 1 sprig

name: Black Forest
description: A coffee-forward cocktail from the Angostura Professional Mixing Guide. Benedictine and maraschino liqueur blend with chilled coffee for a drink reminiscent of Black Forest cake.
ingredients:
Coffee – 60 ml
 Benedictine – 7 ml
 Maraschino Liqueur – 7 ml

name: Black Russian
description: A classic coffee and vodka drink from 1949, attributed to Belgian barkeep Gustave Tops of the Hotel Metropole in Brussels. Vodka is poured over ice, followed by coffee liqueur.
ingredients:
Vodka – 60 ml
 Coffee Liqueur – 30 ml

name: Blackthorne
description: Rich and smooth, the Blackthorne was invented by famed American expatriate Harry Craddock at the Savoy Hotel in London during Prohibition. It pairs Irish whiskey with sweet vermouth and bitters.
ingredients:
Irish Whiskey – 60 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 30 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 2 dash
 Lemon Peel – 1

name: Bloody Mary
description: A classic "hair of the dog" brunch cocktail with disputed origins from the early 20th century. Its base of vodka and tomato juice is spiced with Worcestershire, hot sauce, and horseradish.
ingredients:
Lime – 1 wedge
 Salt
 Vodka – 45 ml
 Tomato Juice – 90 ml
 Lemon Juice – 30 ml
 Worcestershire Sauce – 2.5 ml
 Hot Sauce – 3 drop
 Celery Salt – 1.0 ml
 Horseradish – 7 ml

name: Blue Bird
description: A gin-based cocktail created in 1927 by Frank Meier at the Ritz in Paris to celebrate the automobile speed record set by Sir Malcolm Campbell in his famous Blue Bird racing car.
ingredients:
Gin – 40 ml
 Triple Sec – 10 ml
 Blue Curacao – 5 ml
 Lemon Juice – 15 ml

name: Bobby Burns
description: A classic cocktail named for Scottish poet Robert Burns. Equal parts Scotch and sweet vermouth are enhanced with a barspoon of herbal Benedictine and a lemon peel garnish.
ingredients:
Scotch Whiskey – 45 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 45 ml
 Benedictine – 5.0 ml
 Lemon Peel – 1

name: Bocce Ball
description: A fruit-forward, simple, easy-drinking cocktail named for the Italian lawn game. Amaretto and orange juice are lengthened with club soda for a refreshing, low-proof afternoon sipper.
ingredients:
Amaretto – 45 ml
 Orange Juice – 45 ml
 Club Soda – 120 ml
 Orange – 1 slice

name: Bols Old Fashioned
description: A twist on the classic Old Fashioned using Bols Barrel Aged Genever, whose malty taste is similar to bourbon. Simple syrup and Angostura bitters complete this spirit-forward Dutch variation.
ingredients:
Bols Barrel Aged Genever – 60 ml
 Simple Syrup – 10 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 2 dashes
 Cherry – 1

name: Boulevardier
description: Cousin to the Negroni, substituting bourbon for gin. First appeared in Harry McElhone's 1927 Barflies & Cocktails. Named after a Paris magazine edited by American expatriate Erskine Gwynn.
ingredients:
Bourbon – 45 ml
 Campari – 30 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 30 ml
 Orange Peel – 1 twist
 Cherry – 1

name: Boulevardier Rye
description: A variant of the Boulevardier using rye whiskey instead of bourbon for a spicier, more assertive take. The rye's peppery notes pair beautifully with Campari and sweet vermouth.
ingredients:
Rye – 45 ml
 Campari – 30 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 30 ml
 Orange Peel – 1 twist
 Cherry – 1

name: Brainstorm
description: A cocktail similar to the Manhattan, getting its sweetness and complexity from Benedictine liqueur. Bourbon and dry vermouth provide the backbone, finished with an orange peel twist.
ingredients:
Bourbon – 45 ml
 Dry Vermouth – 30 ml
 Benedictine – 20 ml
 Orange Peel – 1 twist

name: Brandy Crusta
description: A cocktail originating at the New Orleans City Exchange under Joseph Santini in the 1850s. Named for its sugar-crusted rim, it features cognac, orange curaçao, and a touch of lemon.
ingredients:
Lemon – 1 wedge
 Granulated Sugar
 Cognac – 60 ml
 Orange Curacao – 5.0 ml
 Lemon Juice – 2.5 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 1 dash
 Lemon Peel – 1

name: Brandy Milk Punch
description: A popular New Orleans brunch cocktail, this delicious and decadent drink blends cognac with milk, simple syrup, and vanilla extract, topped with a dusting of fresh nutmeg.
ingredients:
Cognac – 60 ml
 Milk – 120 ml
 Simple Syrup – 7 ml
 Vanilla Extract – 2.5 ml
 Nutmeg – 1 pinch

name: Brave Bull
description: Essentially a Black Russian with tequila in place of vodka. The flavor combination of tequila and coffee liqueur works surprisingly well, yielding a rich, spirit-forward nightcap.
ingredients:
Tequila – 30 ml
 Coffee Liqueur – 30 ml

name: Breizh Mule
description: A Breton variation of the Moscow Mule using buckwheat-flavored gin from Saint-Malo instead of vodka. The gin's toasty, nutty character adds depth to the classic lime and ginger beer combo.
ingredients:
Malouins Gin – 60 ml
 Lime Juice – 15 ml
 Ginger Beer – 120 ml

name: Bronx
description: Essentially a Martini with orange juice and sweet vermouth, hugely popular right after Prohibition. Its inventor is disputed between Johnnie Solon at the Waldorf-Astoria and a Bronx restaurateur.
ingredients:
Gin – 60 ml
 Orange Juice – 30 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 15 ml
 Dry Vermouth – 15 ml

name: Brookstone
description: Designed by cocktail consultant Jacob Grier, sweetened with maple syrup as a nod to New Hampshire heritage. Aged rum and grade B maple syrup make this drink exceptional.
ingredients:
Aged Rum – 60 ml
 Lime Juice – 20 ml
 Maple Syrup – 20 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 1 dash
 Lime – 1 wedge

name: Bushwacker
description: A creamy tropical cocktail blending coffee liqueur, Irish cream, light rum, and amaretto with cream. This version from Stew Ellington's 901 Very Good Cocktails is very easy going.
ingredients:
Coffee Liqueur – 15 ml
 Irish Cream – 15 ml
 Light Rum – 15 ml
 Amaretto – 15 ml
 Cream – 60 ml

name: Buster Brown
description: Most likely named after the comic strip character from the early 1900s, the Buster Brown is a classic pre-Prohibition bourbon sour with lemon juice, simple syrup, and orange bitters.
ingredients:
Bourbon – 45 ml
 Lemon Juice – 15 ml
 Simple Syrup – 7 ml
 Orange Bitters – 2 dash

name: Cable Car
description: Adapted from a cocktail at the Starlight Room in San Francisco. Spiced rum and triple sec are shaken with lemon juice, egg white, and simple syrup, served in a cinnamon-sugar-rimmed glass.
ingredients:
Lemon – 1 wedge
 Cinnamon Sugar
 Egg white – 15 ml
 Spiced Rum – 60 ml
 Triple Sec – 30 ml
 Lemon Juice – 15 ml
 Simple Syrup – 7 ml

name: Caipirinha
description: The national drink of Brazil, made from cachaça, a spirit distilled from fermented sugar cane juice. Muddled lime and sugar complete this simple yet iconic cocktail now popular worldwide.
ingredients:
Granulated Sugar – 20 g
 Lime – 2 quarter
 Cachaca – 60 ml

name: Caipiroska
description: A variation on the Caipirinha using vodka instead of cachaça, this drink has grown popular in South America as premium vodkas have become more available in that region.
ingredients:
Granulated Sugar – 20 g
 Lime – 2 wedge
 Vodka – 60 ml

name: Cameron's Kick
description: An odd combination of ingredients that somehow comes together quite nicely. Adapted from The Savoy Cocktail Book, it blends Scotch and Irish whiskey with lemon juice and orgeat syrup.
ingredients:
Scotch Whiskey – 30 ml
 Irish Whiskey – 30 ml
 Lemon Juice – 15 ml
 Orgeat Syrup – 15 ml

name: Cape Cod Cocktail
description: Originally called the "Red Devil" when created by the Ocean Spray cooperative in 1945. A simple mix of vodka and cranberry juice, it is related to the Sea Breeze and Bay Breeze cocktails.
ingredients:
Cranberry Juice – 120 ml
 Vodka – 60 ml
 Lime – 1 wedge

name: Champagne Mojito
description: A sparkling spin on the classic mojito. Muddled mint, lime, and light rum form the base, topped with sparkling wine for an effervescent, celebratory Caribbean-inspired cocktail.
ingredients:
Mint – 8 leaf
 Raw Sugar – 4 g
 Lime Juice – 20 ml
 Light Rum – 60 ml
 Simple Syrup – 15 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 1 dash
 Sparkling Wine – 60 ml
 Lime – 1 wheel
 Mint – 1 sprig

name: Charlie Chaplin
description: Created at the Waldorf Astoria in NYC in the 1920s and named after the legendary silent film star. Equal parts apricot liqueur, sloe gin, and lime juice make a fruity, balanced cocktail.
ingredients:
Apricot Liqueur – 30 ml
 Sloe Gin – 30 ml
 Lime Juice – 30 ml

name: Chingon
description: Created by Brian Miller of Death & Company in 2009 and named after a Mexican slang term for cool. Tequila reposado meets Benedictine, citrus juices, and orgeat for a complex sipper.
ingredients:
Tequila Reposado – 60 ml
 Benedictine – 7 ml
 Orange Juice – 15 ml
 Lime Juice – 15 ml
 Orgeat Syrup – 15 ml
 Orange Peel – 1 twist

name: Christmas Moscow Mule
description: A festive variation of the popular Moscow Mule that swaps ginger beer for cranberry juice and spiced red berries syrup, giving a warm holiday spirit to this beloved classic.
ingredients:
Vodka – 60 ml
 Lime Juice – 15 ml
 Spiced Red Berries Syrup – 10 ml
 Cranberry Juice – 60 ml

name: Chrysanthemum
description: A low-alcohol cocktail created around 1916 by Hugo Ensslin, a German bartender in New York. Dry vermouth and Benedictine with a dash of absinthe make an elegant, herbal aperitif.
ingredients:
Dry Vermouth – 60 ml
 Benedictine – 30 ml
 Absinthe – 3 dash
 Orange Peel – 1

name: Clipper
description: Peggy Guggenheim's biography mentions this cocktail was served during the 1940s on the Boeing flying boats known as Clippers. Light rum, dry vermouth, and grenadine make a smooth sipper.
ingredients:
Light Rum – 45 ml
 Dry Vermouth – 45 ml
 Grenadine – 10 ml

name: Cloister
description: An herbal gin sour dating back at least to the 1970s. Yellow Chartreuse adds alpine complexity to gin, grapefruit juice, and lemon juice for a refreshing, layered citrus cocktail.
ingredients:
Gin – 45 ml
 Yellow Chartreuse – 15 ml
 Grapefruit Juice – 15 ml
 Lemon Juice – 7 ml
 Simple Syrup – 7 ml

name: Coffee Boulevardier
description: A twist on the classic Boulevardier where coffee-infused Campari replaces the original, adding roasted depth. Bourbon and the bitter coffee notes create a bold, sophisticated variation.
ingredients:
Bourbon – 45 ml
 Coffee-infused Campari – 30 ml

name: Coffee Cocktail
description: Despite its name, this classic drink contains no coffee at all. A whole egg, brandy, ruby port, and simple syrup are shaken to create a rich, velvety drink topped with nutmeg.
ingredients:
Egg – 45 ml
 Brandy – 45 ml
 Ruby Port – 45 ml
 Simple Syrup – 15 ml
 Nutmeg – 1 pinch

name: Coffee Negroni
description: A modern twist on the 1919 Negroni, replacing standard Campari with coffee-infused Campari. The roasted coffee notes add complexity to the classic combination of gin and sweet vermouth.
ingredients:
Gin – 30 ml
 Coffee-infused Campari – 30 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 30 ml
 Orange Peel – 1

name: Cognac Sazerac
description: Early Sazeracs were made with cognac until the Phylloxera epidemic wiped out French vineyards in the 1870s. Rye was substituted and the change stuck, but this is the original version.
ingredients:
Cognac – 60 ml
 Simple Syrup – 15 ml
 Peychaud's Bitters – 3 dash

name: Colorado Bulldog
description: Very similar to a White Russian, this simple fizzy cocktail blends vodka, coffee liqueur, and cream. Its unknown origins haven't diminished its popularity as a creamy, easy-drinking treat.
ingredients:
Vodka – 30 ml
 Coffee Liqueur – 30 ml
 Cream – 30 ml

name: Corpse Reviver #2
description: From Harry Craddock's 1930 Savoy Cocktail Handbook, this "hair of the dog" hangover cure combines gin, Cointreau, Lillet Blanc, and lemon juice with a dash of absinthe.
ingredients:
Gin – 20 ml
 Cointreau – 20 ml
 Lillet Blanc – 20 ml
 Lemon Juice – 20 ml
 Absinthe – 1 dash

name: Cosmopolitan
description: The Cosmo, a relative of cranberry juice cocktails, is claimed by several 1970s bartenders. It became extremely popular in the 1990s after frequent mentions in Sex and the City.
ingredients:
Vodka – 60 ml
 Triple Sec – 20 ml
 Lime Juice – 15 ml
 Cranberry Juice – 15 ml
 Orange Peel – 1

name: Cranberry Margarita
description: A variation on the classic Margarita adding cranberry juice to the mix. Tequila, triple sec, lime juice, and cranberry make a tart, fruity twist on the beloved Mexican classic.
ingredients:
Lime – 1 wedge
 Kosher Salt
 Tequila – 45 ml
 Triple Sec – 30 ml
 Cranberry Juice – 30 ml
 Lime Juice – 15 ml
 Lime – 1 wheel

name: Crux
description: An equal-portions drink that comes together very nicely. Cognac, Dubonnet, triple sec, and lemon juice are combined in equal measure for a balanced, citrus-accented cocktail.
ingredients:
Cognac – 20 ml
 Dubonnet – 20 ml
 Triple Sec – 20 ml
 Lemon Juice – 20 ml
 Orange Peel – 1 twist

name: Daiquiri
description: Reportedly invented by American mining engineer Jennings Cox in late 1800s Cuba. Its popularity exploded in the 1940s during wartime rum availability. A simple blend of rum, lime, and sugar.
ingredients:
Light Rum – 60 ml
 Lime Juice – 20 ml
 Simple Syrup – 20 ml
 Lime – 1 wedge

name: Daisy
description: The Brandy Daisy is the precursor to both the Sidecar and Margarita (margarita is Spanish for daisy). Popular in the late 1800s, first published in Jerry Thomas' 1876 Bartender's Guide.
ingredients:
Brandy – 60 ml
 Lemon Juice – 15 ml
 Grenadine – 5.0 ml
 Simple Syrup – 5.0 ml
 Lemon – 1 wedge

name: Darb
description: An adaptation of a cocktail from The Savoy Cocktail Book. Equal parts gin, apricot liqueur, and dry vermouth are brightened with a splash of lemon juice for a fruity, balanced drink.
ingredients:
Gin – 20 ml
 Apricot Liqueur – 20 ml
 Dry Vermouth – 20 ml
 Lemon Juice – 7 ml

name: Dark 'N Stormy
description: Created and trademarked by the makers of Gosling's Black Seal Rum, this mix of dark rum and ginger beer with lime juice has been called the national drink of Bermuda.
ingredients:
Dark Rum – 45 ml
 Lime Juice – 15 ml
 Ginger Beer – 120 ml
 Lime – 1 wedge

name: Death In The Afternoon
description: A cocktail by Ernest Hemingway, contributed to the 1935 book So Red The Nose. Simply absinthe topped with chilled sparkling wine, this potent drink is sometimes called a Hemingway.
ingredients:
Absinthe – 20 ml
 Sparkling Wine – 150 ml

name: Derby
description: A relatively uncommon pairing of bourbon and lime juice, the Derby was named after the Kentucky Derby horse race and appeared in Trader Vic's 1947 Bartenders Guide.
ingredients:
Bourbon – 30 ml
 Lime Juice – 20 ml
 Orange Curacao – 15 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 15 ml
 Mint – 1 leaf

name: Diamondback
description: A cocktail from Ted Saucier's 1962 Bottoms Up! book. Rye whiskey, applejack, and yellow Chartreuse combine for a spirit-forward drink with herbal and apple notes. Garnished with a cherry.
ingredients:
Rye – 45 ml
 Applejack – 20 ml
 Yellow Chartreuse – 20 ml
 Cherry – 1

name: Diplomat
description: A vermouth-based cocktail that's not too heavy on alcohol, ideal for keeping a clear head during important diplomatic negotiations. Equal parts dry and sweet vermouth with maraschino and bitters.
ingredients:
Dry Vermouth – 45 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 45 ml
 Maraschino Liqueur – 1 dash
 Angostura Bitters – 1 dash
 Cherry – 1

name: Division Bell
description: From Mayahuel restaurant in New York, famed for its elevation of mezcal drinks to an art form. Named after the Pink Floyd album, it combines mezcal, Aperol, lime, and maraschino.
ingredients:
Mezcal – 30 ml
 Aperol – 20 ml
 Lime Juice – 20 ml
 Maraschino Liqueur – 15 ml
 Grapefruit Peel – 1

name: Dry Rob Roy
description: Created at the Waldorf Astoria in 1894 to honor the operetta Rob Roy, based on the Scottish hero. Similar to a Manhattan but made with Scotch and dry vermouth instead of bourbon and sweet vermouth.
ingredients:
Scotch Whiskey – 75 ml
 Dry Vermouth – 30 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 2 dash
 Lemon Peel – 1

name: Dubonnet Cocktail
description: A popular English cocktail from the 1920s. Equal parts Dubonnet and gin are stirred with Angostura bitters and garnished with a lemon twist for a bittersweet, aromatic aperitif.
ingredients:
Dubonnet – 45 ml
 Gin – 45 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 2 dash
 Lemon Peel – 1 twist

name: Eastern Standard - SoHo House Tonic
description: One of the Soho House group's House Tonic cocktails, the Eastern Standard is based on the Eastside, a cucumber and mint gin drink inspired by the classic Southside cocktail.
ingredients:
Cucumber - 3 slices
 Simple Syrup - 10 ml
 Gin - 50 ml
 Lime Juice - 10 ml
 Mint - 2 leaf
 Cucumber - 1 slice

name: El Camino
description: An intriguing spirit-forward cocktail from The Chestnut Club in Santa Monica, California. Mezcal and rye are united by herbal Benedictine and Peychaud's bitters, with an orange twist.
ingredients:
Mezcal – 30 ml
 Rye – 30 ml
 Benedictine – 15 ml
 Peychaud's Bitters – 4 dash
 Orange Peel – 1 twist

name: El Diablo
description: First referenced in a 1946 Trader Vic's book, this is one of the few tequila drinks from that era. Its brilliant red color from crème de cassis likely inspired the devilish name.
ingredients:
Tequila Blanco – 60 ml
 Lime Juice – 30 ml
 Creme de Cassis – 30 ml
 Ginger Beer – 120 ml
 Lime – 1 wedge

name: El Floridita No. 1
description: Named after the Floridita bar in Havana, this was rumored to be Ernest Hemingway's favorite. The bar's bartenders specialized in rum and citrus cocktails like this rum-lime-maraschino blend.
ingredients:
Light Rum – 60 ml
 Lime Juice – 30 ml
 Simple Syrup – 15 ml
 Maraschino Liqueur – 15 ml
 Lime – 1 wedge

name: El Presidente
description: A classic Cuban cocktail named after president Gerardo Machado in the 1920s. Light rum, bianco vermouth, orange curaçao, and a touch of grenadine make this elegant pre-dinner drink.
ingredients:
Light Rum – 45 ml
 Bianco Vermouth – 30 ml
 Orange Curacao – 5.0 ml
 Grenadine – 2.5 ml
 Cherry – 1

name: Elderflower Margarita
description: A variant of the classic Margarita using St. Germain elderflower liqueur in place of triple sec. The floral elderflower adds elegance and sweetness to the tequila and lime combination.
ingredients:
Lime – 1 wedge
 Kosher Salt
 Tequila – 45 ml
 St. Germain – 30 ml
 Lime Juice – 20 ml
 Lime – 1 wheel

name: Electric Kamikaze
description: A shooter named for its electric blue appearance from blue curaçao, though its fruity flavor is all citrus. Triple sec, vodka, and lime juice complete this refreshing but potent shot.
ingredients:
Triple Sec – 15 ml
 Vodka – 15 ml
 Blue Curacao – 15 ml
 Lime Juice – 15 ml

name: Final Ward
description: A variation on the Last Word by New York bartender Phil Ward. He swapped gin for rye and lime for lemon, letting the interplay of maraschino and green Chartreuse shine through.
ingredients:
Rye – 20 ml
 Lemon Juice – 20 ml
 Green Chartreuse – 20 ml
 Maraschino Liqueur – 20 ml

name: Fourth Degree
description: Yet another cocktail from the tremendously influential Savoy Cocktail Book. Equal parts gin, dry vermouth, and sweet vermouth are accented with a dash of absinthe and a lemon peel.
ingredients:
Gin – 20 ml
 Dry Vermouth – 20 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 20 ml
 Absinthe – 5.0 ml
 Lemon Peel – 1

name: Freddie Fudpucker
description: A tequila-based variation of the classic Harvey Wallbanger. Tequila replaces vodka, mixed with orange juice and floated Galliano liqueur, for a fruity, vanilla-accented tall drink.
ingredients:
Tequila – 30 ml
 Orange Juice – 120 ml
 Galliano – 15 ml
 Lemon Peel – 1

name: French 75
description: Created in 1915 at the New York Bar in Paris. Named after the French 75mm artillery cannon, a reference to the "kick" of this gin, lemon juice, simple syrup, and sparkling wine cocktail.
ingredients:
Gin – 30 ml
 Lemon Juice – 30 ml
 Simple Syrup – 15 ml
 Sparkling Wine – 60 ml
 Lemon Peel – 1

name: Garden Party
description: Perfectly at home at the event it was named for. Green Chartreuse, muddled mint, lime juice, and club soda combine for an herbal, refreshing drink ideal for a hot summer day.
ingredients:
Mint – 5 leaf
 Lime Juice – 15 ml
 Simple Syrup – 7 ml
 Green Chartreuse – 45 ml
 Club Soda – 75 ml
 Mint – 1 sprig

name: Gimlet
description: A classic cocktail of gin and lime juice, similar to a daiquiri but with gin. As described in Raymond Chandler's The Long Goodbye: half gin and half lime juice and nothing else.
ingredients:
Gin – 60 ml
 Lime Juice – 30 ml
 Simple Syrup – 20 ml

name: Gin Genie
description: A visually striking layered drink from expert mixologist Wayne Collins, named after David Bowie's song The Jean Genie. Gin and sloe gin are combined with lemon, simple syrup, and muddled mint.
ingredients:
Mint – 8 leaf
 Gin – 30 ml
 Lemon Juice – 30 ml
 Simple Syrup – 15 ml
 Sloe Gin – 30 ml

name: Gin Rickey
description: Originally made with bourbon by Col. Joe Rickey at Shoomakers bar in Washington, D.C. in the 1880s. The gin version may have originated at the 1893 Chicago Exposition and has become the standard.
ingredients:
Gin - 60 ml
 Lime Juice - 15 ml
 Spent Lime Hull - 1
 Club Soda - 120 ml

name: Ginger Champagne
description: A way to spice up your champagne with ginger and vodka! A splash of vodka and ginger syrup adds warmth and complexity to sparkling wine, perfect for the holiday season.
ingredients:
Vodka – 10 ml
 Ginger Syrup – 20 ml
 Sparkling Wine – 100 ml
 Candied Ginger – 3

name: Ginger Spritz
description: The illegitimate child of a Spritz and a Moscow Mule. Lillet Blanc, sparkling wine, and ginger beer combine for a light, effervescent aperitif with a spicy ginger kick.
ingredients:
Lillet Blanc – 40 ml
 Sparkling Wine – 60 ml
 Ginger Beer – 20 ml

name: Godfather
description: The origins of this drink are unknown, but the unlikely pairing of Scotch whisky and amaretto works better than one might expect. The almond sweetness softens the Scotch's smoky bite.
ingredients:
Scotch Whiskey – 60 ml
 Amaretto – 30 ml

name: Godmother
description: A variation on the Godfather cocktail using vodka in place of Scotch. The neutral spirit lets the sweet almond flavor of amaretto take center stage in this simple two-ingredient drink.
ingredients:
Vodka – 60 ml
 Amaretto – 30 ml

name: Happy Honey
description: According to Philippe Collin's 2024 novel Le Barman du Ritz, this cocktail was invented by Charles, one of Frank Meier's students, who became owner of the Hôtel du Palais in Biarritz.
ingredients:
Brandy – 50 ml
 Grapefruit Juice – 25 ml
 Honey – 7 ml
 Hot Sauce – 2 dash

name: Harvard Cooler
description: Slate's Troy Patterson ranked this the best Ivy League-named cocktail. A refreshing twist on the Collins, using Calvados as the base spirit with lime juice, simple syrup, and club soda.
ingredients:
Calvados – 60 ml
 Lime Juice – 20 ml
 Simple Syrup – 15 ml
 Club Soda – 120 ml
 Lime – 1 wedge

name: Harvey Wallbanger
description: Invented in 1952 by Donato "Duke" Antone, who started the first American mixology school in California. Vodka and orange juice are topped with a float of Italian Galliano liqueur.
ingredients:
Vodka – 30 ml
 Orange Juice – 120 ml
 Galliano – 15 ml
 Lemon Peel – 1

name: Hemingway Daiquiri
description: A friendlier version of a Hemingway favorite, similar to the El Floridita but with the addition of grapefruit juice. Light rum, lime, maraschino liqueur, and grapefruit make it tangy and bright.
ingredients:
Light Rum – 60 ml
 Lime Juice – 20 ml
 Maraschino Liqueur – 15 ml
 Grapefruit Juice – 15 ml
 Lime – 1 wedge

name: Honeymoon
description: A wonderful cocktail from Hugo Ensslin's 1917 Recipes for Mixed Drinks. Calvados, Benedictine, orange curaçao, and lemon juice combine for a beautifully balanced, aromatic celebration drink.
ingredients:
Calvados – 30 ml
 Lemon Juice – 15 ml
 Benedictine – 15 ml
 Orange Curacao – 15 ml
 Lemon Peel – 1 twist

name: Hot Caipirinha
description: In Germany, the biggest market for cachaça outside Brazil, drinkers adapt the Caipirinha for cold weather by serving it hot. Choosing a barrel-aged cachaça makes this odd combination work well.
ingredients:
Lime – 2 quarter
 Simple Syrup – 30 ml
 Cachaca – 60 ml
 Water – 150 ml

name: Hugo Spritz
description: A lighter variation of the Spritz popular in Austria and northern Italy. St. Germain elderflower liqueur replaces the traditional Aperol, combined with sparkling wine and a splash of club soda.
ingredients:
St. Germain – 40 ml
 Sparkling Wine – 60 ml
 Club Soda – 20 ml

name: Hummingbird
description: A refreshing take on the Hummingbird from Brooks Reitz of FIG in Charleston, South Carolina. Gin, Campari, St. Germain, and sweet vermouth make a bittersweet, floral, and complex aperitif.
ingredients:
Gin – 20 ml
 Campari – 20 ml
 St. Germain – 15 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 7 ml
 Orange – 1 wheel

name: In The Neighborhood
description: This signature drink was served to celebrities and VIPs at the 2015 Neighborhood Awards hosted by Steve Harvey. Aged rum, lime, maple syrup, and bitters make a smooth, balanced cocktail.
ingredients:
Aged Rum – 60 ml
 Lime Juice – 30 ml
 Maple Syrup – 20 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 1 dash
 Lime Peel – 1 twist

name: Incognito
description: A variation on a cocktail from the Cafe Royal Cocktail Book, credited to J. E. Johnson. Cognac, dry vermouth, and apricot liqueur are combined with Angostura bitters and a lemon twist.
ingredients:
Cognac – 45 ml
 Dry Vermouth – 45 ml
 Apricot Liqueur – 30 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 3 dash
 Lemon Peel – 1 twist

name: Jack Rose
description: A classic dating back to the 1920s that appears in Hemingway's The Sun Also Rises and David Embury's The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks. Applejack, lemon juice, and grenadine create a rosy hue.
ingredients:
Applejack – 60 ml
 Lemon Juice – 30 ml
 Grenadine – 20 ml
 Lemon Peel – 1 twist

name: Jacob's Rainbow
description: A carefully layered cocktail that tastes much better once stirred to blend the flavors. Grenadine, blue curaçao, citrus juices, and dark rum create a colorful, fruity showstopper.
ingredients:
Grenadine – 30 ml
 Blue Curacao – 30 ml
 Orange Juice – 60 ml
 Lime Juice – 15 ml
 Pineapple Juice – 60 ml
 Dark Rum – 30 ml

name: Jamaican Mule
description: A Caribbean twist on the Moscow Mule, swapping vodka for dark rum. The rum's molasses richness pairs naturally with spicy ginger beer and fresh lime juice for a tropical, full-bodied mule.
ingredients:
Dark Rum – 60 ml
 Lime Juice – 15 ml
 Ginger Beer – 120 ml

name: James Joyce
description: Named for Ireland's most famous author and based on the Oriental cocktail. Irish whiskey is paired with sweet vermouth, Cointreau, and lime juice for a citrusy, literary libation.
ingredients:
Irish Whiskey – 45 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 20 ml
 Cointreau – 20 ml
 Lime Juice – 15 ml

name: Jasmine
description: Created by Paul Harrington in the mid-1990s at the Townhouse Bar & Grill in Emeryville, California. Gin, triple sec, Campari, and lemon juice yield a bittersweet, citrus-forward cocktail.
ingredients:
Gin – 45 ml
 Triple Sec – 30 ml
 Campari – 20 ml
 Lemon Juice – 15 ml

name: JM Amaretto Sour
description: From Jeffrey Morgenthaler of Clyde Common in Portland, Oregon, who calls this the "best Amaretto Sour you've ever had." Cask-strength bourbon helps balance the sweetness of the amaretto.
ingredients:
Egg white – 15 ml
 Amaretto – 45 ml
 Lemon Juice – 30 ml
 Bourbon – 20 ml
 Simple Syrup – 7 ml
 Lemon Peel – 1 twist
 Cherry – 1

name: Jubilant
description: A smooth, refreshing, herbal cocktail from The Cafe Royal Cocktail Book. Gin and Benedictine are shaken with lemon and orange juice and egg white for a silky, frothy, elegant drink.
ingredients:
Egg white – 15 ml
 Gin – 45 ml
 Benedictine – 20 ml
 Lemon Juice – 15 ml
 Orange Juice – 15 ml
 Orange – 1 slice

name: Kalê Sour
description: A tailor-made cocktail recipe created and shared by a fellow home mixologist. Kalê liqueur, lemon juice, and egg white are shaken vigorously to produce a silky, frothy sour cocktail.
ingredients:
Kalê Liqueur – 60 ml
 Lemon Juice – 30 ml
 Egg white – 30 g

name: Kamikaze
description: No verified origin exists for the Kamikaze, but most accounts place it in the mid-1970s via a Smirnoff advertisement. Vodka, triple sec, and lime juice make a sharp, citrusy shooter or cocktail.
ingredients:
Vodka – 60 ml
 Triple Sec – 30 ml
 Lime Juice – 15 ml
 Lime – 1 wedge

name: Kilmoon Sour
description: A Celtic variation on the whisky sour created for the release of mystery novel Kilmoon by Lisa Alber. Irish whiskey and grenadine add a red hue suggestive of its murderous origin.
ingredients:
Irish Whiskey – 60 ml
 Lemon Juice – 20 ml
 Grenadine – 15 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 2 dash
 Blood Orange – 1 wheel

name: Kir Royale
description: The Kir Royale is a standard Kir made with sparkling wine instead of white wine. Usually served in France as an apéritif, a splash of crème de cassis adds deep berry sweetness to the bubbly.
ingredients:
Creme de Cassis – 15 ml
 Sparkling Wine – 150 ml
 Lemon – 1 twist

name: La Louisiane
description: According to Stanley Arthur's 1937 "Famous New Orleans Drinks," this was the house cocktail of the Restaurant de la Louisiane. Equal parts rye, sweet vermouth, and Benedictine.
ingredients:
Rye – 20 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 20 ml
 Benedictine – 20 ml
 Absinthe – 3 dash
 Peychaud's Bitters – 3 dash
 Cherry – 1

name: Lagerita
description: A beery version of a margarita that works best with a light Mexican beer. From cocktail expert Simon Ford, it combines tequila, Cointreau, and lime juice, topped off with cold lager.
ingredients:
Lime
 Salt
 Ice
 Tequila – 60 ml
 Cointreau – 20 ml
 Lime Juice – 30 ml
 Lime – 1 wheel

name: Last Word
description: A Prohibition-era cocktail from the Detroit Athletic Club, lost after WWII. Rediscovered in 2004 by Murray Stenson at Seattle's Zig Zag Cafe. Equal parts gin, Chartreuse, lime, and maraschino.
ingredients:
Gin – 20 ml
 Green Chartreuse – 20 ml
 Lime Juice – 20 ml
 Maraschino Liqueur – 20 ml

name: Leatherneck
description: Created in 1951 by columnist Frank Farrell, a former U.S. Marine ("Leatherneck" is Marine slang). Canadian whiskey and blue curaçao give this cocktail its distinctive color and citrus notes.
ingredients:
Canadian Whiskey – 60 ml
 Blue Curacao – 30 ml
 Lime Juice – 15 ml
 Lime – 1 wheel

name: Lonely Bull
description: A tequila-based variation on the White Russian, replacing vodka with tequila. Equal parts tequila, coffee liqueur, and cream blend into a smooth, rich after-dinner cocktail.
ingredients:
Tequila – 30 ml
 Coffee Liqueur – 30 ml
 Cream – 30 ml

name: Long Island Iced Tea
description: Approach with caution! Famous for tasting just like iced tea while being mostly liquor. Vodka, rum, gin, tequila, and triple sec are topped with a splash of cola for a deceptively smooth drink.
ingredients:
Vodka – 20 ml
 Light Rum – 20 ml
 Gin – 20 ml
 Tequila Blanco – 20 ml
 Triple Sec – 20 ml
 Simple Syrup – 15 ml
 Cola – 30 ml
 Lemon – 1 wedge

name: Madras
description: Perhaps an evolution of the Cape Cod Cocktail, the Madras adds orange juice to the cranberry-vodka base for a more complex, fruity drink. Refreshing and easy to make at home.
ingredients:
Cranberry Juice – 45 ml
 Vodka – 60 ml
 Orange Juice – 60 ml
 Orange – 1 wheel

name: Mai Tai
description: Reportedly created by Victor Bergeron of Trader Vic's in 1944. Its name derives from the Tahitian "Maita'i roa ae!" meaning "Very good!" It became synonymous with 1950s and 1960s tiki culture.
ingredients:
Orgeat Syrup – 20 ml
 Triple Sec – 15 ml
 Lime Juice – 30 ml
 Light Rum – 30 ml
 Dark Rum – 30 ml
 Cherry – 1

name: Mamie Taylor
description: One of the most popular cocktails of the late 1800s, named after a Broadway singer of that era. Similar to the Moscow Mule and the Dark and Stormy, it features Scotch and ginger beer.
ingredients:
Scotch Whiskey – 45 ml
 Lime Juice – 15 ml
 Ginger Beer – 120 ml

name: Manhattan
description: Its precise origin is debatable, with some tracing it to the Manhattan Club in NYC in the 1870s. Traditionally made with rye, many recipes now use bourbon with sweet vermouth and bitters.
ingredients:
Bourbon – 60 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 30 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 2 dash
 Cherry – 1

name: Manhattan Rye
description: The original Manhattan recipe calls for rye whiskey, offering a spicier, drier flavor profile than the bourbon version. Rye, sweet vermouth, and Angostura bitters make a timeless classic.
ingredients:
Rye – 60 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 30 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 2 dash
 Cherry – 1

name: Maple Bourbon Old Fashioned
description: A seasonal take on the Old Fashioned, substituting maple syrup for sugar. The maple gives the bourbon a smoother, earthier flavor, complemented by Angostura bitters and orange peel.
ingredients:
Angostura Bitters – 3 dash
 Maple Syrup – 15 ml
 Bourbon – 60 ml
 Orange Peel – 1 twist

name: Maple Leaf
description: A wonderful fall cocktail and a variation on the whiskey sour. Maple syrup takes the place of sugar as the sweetener, pairing beautifully with bourbon and lemon juice for autumn sipping.
ingredients:
Bourbon – 45 ml
 Lemon Juice – 15 ml
 Maple Syrup – 15 ml

name: Margarita
description: The most common tequila cocktail in the US, often attributed to Don Carlo Orozco at Hussong's Cantina in Ensenada in 1941. Margarita is Spanish for daisy, linking it to an older brandy drink.
ingredients:
Lime – 1 wedge
 Kosher Salt
 Tequila Blanco – 45 ml
 Triple Sec – 20 ml
 Lime Juice – 15 ml

name: Martini
description: Probably the best-known cocktail of all time, the Martini evolved from the 1860s-era Martinez. The ideal gin-to-vermouth ratio is endlessly debated; this recipe uses a classic 3:1 proportion.
ingredients:
Gin – 75 ml
 Dry Vermouth – 20 ml
 Green Olive – 1

name: Ménage à trois
description: The classic Old Fashioned revisited with a fruity, spicy twist. Belle De Brillet pear liqueur, dark rum, and fresh coffee form a trio in harmony, accented by a dash of Angostura bitters.
ingredients:
Belle de Brillet – 50 ml 
 Dark rum – 20 ml 
 Coffee – 15 ml 
 Angostura Bitters – 1 dash

name: Mezcal Negroni
description: A smoky twist on the classic Negroni, replacing gin with mezcal. The spirit's earthy, smoky character adds a distinctive Mexican dimension to the Campari and sweet vermouth combination.
ingredients:
Mezcal – 30 ml
 Campari – 30 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 30 ml
 Orange Peel – 1

name: Michelada
description: A popular Mexican beer cocktail with many versions throughout Latin America, often used as a hangover remedy. Spiced with hot sauce, Worcestershire, lime, and celery salt over beer.
ingredients:
Cayenne Pepper
 Salt
 Lime Juice – 15 ml
 Hot Sauce – 2 drop
 Worcestershire Sauce – 2 dash
 Black Pepper – 1 pinch
 Celery Salt – 1 pinch
 Lime – 1 wedge

name: Milano Torino #2
description: Milano Torino is the original name of the Americano cocktail, but this version omits the seltzer. Equal parts Campari and sweet vermouth make it a more concentrated, bittersweet variation.
ingredients:
Campari – 60 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 60 ml
 Orange – 1 slice

name: Mimosa
description: Typically served at brunch or to wedding guests. A standard Mimosa is equal parts orange juice and sparkling wine, but this recipe adds Cointreau for a little extra citrus twist.
ingredients:
Cointreau – 15 ml
 Orange Juice – 60 ml
 Sparkling Wine – 120 ml
 Orange – 1 wedge

name: Mint Julep
description: The classic drink of the American South, around since at least 1803. Muddled mint and simple syrup with bourbon over crushed ice. It is famously associated with the Kentucky Derby horse race.
ingredients:
Mint – 10 leaf
 Simple Syrup – 15 ml
 Bourbon – 30 ml
 Mint – 1 sprig

name: Mojito
description: Originating in Cuba and similar to a 19th-century drink called El Draque after privateer Sir Francis Drake. Rum, lime, mint, sugar, and soda make this an enduringly popular refresher.
ingredients:
Simple Syrup – 20 ml
 Mint – 1 sprig
 Light Rum – 60 ml
 Lime Juice – 30 ml
 Club Soda – 90 ml
 Lime – 1 wedge

name: Moscow Mule
description: Created in 1941 by spirits distributor John Martin and Jack Morgan of L.A.'s Cock 'n Bull restaurant. Popular with Hollywood celebrities, it helped popularize vodka in the United States.
ingredients:
Vodka – 60 ml
 Lime Juice – 15 ml
 Ginger Beer – 120 ml

name: Mudslide
description: A creamy cocktail containing three liquors (a Trio). While its exact origins are hard to prove, some of the first Mudslides were served at Rum Point in the Cayman Islands.
ingredients:
Vodka – 60 ml
 Coffee Liqueur – 30 ml
 Irish Cream – 30 ml
 Cream – 30 ml

name: Naked And Famous
description: Inspired by the Last Word and Paper Plane, this drink was created by Joaquín Simó in 2011. Equal parts mezcal, yellow Chartreuse, Aperol, and lime juice create a smoky, balanced cocktail.
ingredients:
Mezcal – 20 ml
 Yellow Chartreuse – 20 ml
 Aperol – 20 ml
 Lime Juice – 20 ml

name: Negroni
description: Created in Florence in 1919 when Count Negroni asked bartender Fosco Scarselli for a stronger Americano with gin instead of soda. Equal parts gin, Campari, and sweet vermouth since then.
ingredients:
Gin – 30 ml
 Campari – 30 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 30 ml
 Orange Peel – 1

name: Negroni Sbagliato
description: Sbagliato means "mistaken" in Italian. Legend says it came from a bartender mistakenly using sparkling wine instead of gin in a Negroni. A happy accident that makes a great aperitif.
ingredients:
Campari – 30 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 30 ml
 Sparkling Wine – 30 ml
 Orange Peel – 1 twist

name: Negroski
description: A variant of the classic Negroni using vodka in place of gin. The neutral spirit allows the bold flavors of Campari and sweet vermouth to take center stage in this simple, bitter-forward twist.
ingredients:
Vodka – 30 ml
 Campari – 30 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 30 ml
 Orange Peel – 1

name: Nevada
description: A great rum drink from Judge Jr.'s 1927 "Here's How" cocktail book. Light rum, grapefruit juice, lime juice, simple syrup, and a dash of Angostura bitters create a citrusy, balanced sour.
ingredients:
Light Rum – 45 ml
 Grapefruit Juice – 30 ml
 Lime Juice – 15 ml
 Simple Syrup – 15 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 1 dash

name: Non-Violette Aviation
description: Created by Hugo Ensslin at the Hotel Wallick in New York (1916). This version follows Harry Craddock's 1930 Savoy recipe, omitting the crème de violette. Gin, lemon, and maraschino liqueur.
ingredients:
Gin – 50 ml
 Lemon Juice – 20 ml
 Maraschino Liqueur – 20 ml
 Cherry – 1

name: Nuts & Berries
description: A unique combination of nut and fruit flavors. Hazelnut-flavored Frangelico and raspberry liqueur are blended with cream for a dessert-like cocktail that's sweet, smooth, and indulgent.
ingredients:
Frangelico – 30 ml
 Raspberry Liqueur – 30 ml
 Cream – 30 ml

name: Nutty Martini
description: This easy-drinking cocktail gets its nutty flavor from Frangelico, an Italian hazelnut liqueur. Combined with vodka, it creates a smooth, dessert-style drink with rich toasted nut notes.
ingredients:
Vodka – 60 ml
 Frangelico – 30 ml

name: Oaxacan Mule
description: A variant of the Moscow Mule using mezcal in place of vodka for an earthier, smokier flavor. Lime juice and ginger beer complement the mezcal's complexity in this Mexican-inspired twist.
ingredients:
Mezcal – 45 ml
 Lime Juice – 15 ml
 Ginger Beer – 60 ml
 Lime – 1 wedge

name: Old Fashioned
description: A classic American drink rooted in sweet whisky cocktails of the 1800s. "Old Fashioned" refers to the original way of making a cocktail: spirit, sugar, bitters, and ice. Simple and timeless.
ingredients:
Bourbon – 60 ml
 Simple Syrup – 15 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 2 dash
 Cherry – 1

name: Oriental
description: Another classic from Harry Craddock's Savoy Cocktail Book. Rye whiskey, sweet vermouth, triple sec, and lime juice combine for a balanced cocktail with citrus and spice character.
ingredients:
Rye – 45 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 20 ml
 Triple Sec – 20 ml
 Lime Juice – 15 ml

name: Paper Plane
description: Created by star New York bartender Sam Ross, named after the M.I.A. song he listened to while inventing it. Equal parts bourbon, lemon juice, Aperol, and amaro make a bittersweet modern classic.
ingredients:
Bourbon – 20 ml
 Lemon Juice – 20 ml
 Aperol – 20 ml
 Amaro – 20 ml

name: Penicillin
description: A modern classic created by New York bartender Sam Ross. Scotch whisky is combined with honey, lemon juice, and ginger for a soothing, warming cocktail that's a cure for a chilly night.
ingredients:
Scotch Whiskey – 50 ml
 Lemon Juice – 20 ml
 Ginger Syrup – 10 ml
 Honey – 5 ml

name: Penja #22
description: A tailor-made cocktail recipe created and shared by a fellow home mixologist. White Penja pepper syrup is combined with beer in this unusual, spice-forward drink from Cameroon's pepper region.
ingredients:
Paragon White Penja Pepper – 15 ml
 Beer – 100 ml

name: Perfect Rob Roy
description: Created at the Waldorf Astoria in 1894 for the operetta Rob Roy. Similar to a Manhattan but with Scotch; the Perfect version uses equal parts sweet and dry vermouth for balanced complexity.
ingredients:
Scotch Whiskey – 75 ml
 Dry Vermouth – 15 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 15 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 2 dash
 Lemon Peel – 1

name: Pimm's Cup
description: A refreshing summer drink popular in Great Britain and closely associated with New Orleans, where it's the house drink at the Napoleon House. Pimm's No. 1, lemon, and ginger ale over ice.
ingredients:
Pimm's No. 1 – 60 ml
 Lemon Juice – 15 ml
 Ginger Ale – 120 ml
 Cucumber – 1 slice

name: Pisco Sour
description: Created in Lima, Peru by American bartender Victor Morris in the early 1920s. The Pisco Sour is the national drink of both Peru and Chile, featuring pisco, lime, egg white, and bitters.
ingredients:
Egg white – 30 ml
 Pisco – 60 ml
 Lime Juice – 20 ml
 Simple Syrup – 15 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 4 dash

name: Pompadour
description: Mentioned in Philippe Collin's 2024 novel Le Barman du Ritz. Created by Frank Meier at the Ritz, it combines Pineau des Charentes, light rum, and lemon juice for an extraordinary flavor.
ingredients:
Pineau des Charentes – 45 ml
 Light Rum – 45 ml
 Lemon Juice – 15 ml

name: Preakness
description: A complex variation on the Manhattan accented with richly herbal Benedictine liqueur. Rye whiskey, sweet vermouth, and Angostura bitters round out this classic horse-racing-themed cocktail.
ingredients:
Rye – 45 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 20 ml
 Benedictine – 7 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 2 dash
 Cherry – 1

name: Queen Bee
description: Created by Neil Kopplin of the Carlyle Restaurant in Portland, Oregon. Vodka, lemon juice, St. Germain, and honey syrup are shaken and topped with sparkling wine for a floral, elegant fizz.
ingredients:
Vodka – 20 ml
 Lemon Juice – 20 ml
 St. Germain – 20 ml
 Honey Syrup – 7 ml
 Sparkling Wine – 30 ml
 Orange Peel – 1

name: Queen Elizabeth
description: Named after its Philadelphia creator's wife, not the queen of England. Dating from just after Prohibition, it combines dry vermouth, Benedictine, and lime juice for an herbal sipper.
ingredients:
Dry Vermouth – 45 ml
 Benedictine – 20 ml
 Lime Juice – 20 ml

name: Rainbow Dee-Lite
description: A layered cocktail from Dee Brun, the Cocktail Diva. After impressing your audience with the stunning visuals of blue curaçao, grenadine, and juices, stir to blend all the flavors.
ingredients:
Blue Curacao – 30 ml
 Vodka – 30 ml
 Grenadine – 30 ml
 Orange Juice – 60 ml
 Pineapple Juice – 30 ml

name: Ramos Gin Fizz
description: Invented by Henry C. Ramos in New Orleans. The key is shaking with ice for 1-2 minutes for a silky texture. Gin, citrus, egg white, cream, and orange flower water create an iconic, frothy drink.
ingredients:
Egg white – 15 ml
 Gin – 60 ml
 Lemon Juice – 15 ml
 Lime Juice – 15 ml
 Simple Syrup – 30 ml
 Orange-Flower Water – 3 drop

name: Raspberry Silver Fizz
description: From Harry Johnson's 1882 New and Improved Bartender's Manual. Gin, raspberry liqueur, lime juice, and egg white are shaken and topped with club soda for a fruity, frothy fizz.
ingredients:
Egg white – 15 ml
 Gin – 45 ml
 Raspberry Liqueur – 15 ml
 Simple Syrup – 8 ml
 Lime Juice – 15 ml
 Club Soda – 60 ml

name: Red Eye
description: A brunch cocktail for those who prefer beer over vodka in their Bloody Marys. Beer, tomato juice, hot sauce, and a raw egg make this hearty eye-opener, inspired by the movie Cocktail.
ingredients:
Beer – 180 ml
 Tomato Juice – 120 ml
 Hot Sauce – 1 dash
 Black Pepper – 1 pinch
 Salt – 1 pinch
 Egg – 1

name: Rhett Butler
description: A refreshing cocktail named after the character in Gone with the Wind. Southern Comfort, orange curaçao, lime juice, and cranberry juice create a fruity, Southern-inspired drink.
ingredients:
Southern Comfort – 30 ml
 Orange Curacao – 30 ml
 Lime Juice – 30 ml
 Cranberry Juice – 60 ml

name: Rhum Swizzle
description: A tasty swizzle from author and former Food & Wine editor Nick Fauchald. Rhum agricole, grapefruit juice, muddled mint, brown sugar syrup, and grenadine make a complex tropical refresher.
ingredients:
Mint – 8 leaf
 Rhum Agricole – 60 ml
 Grapefruit Juice – 60 ml
 Brown Sugar Syrup – 15 ml
 Grenadine – 3.5 ml

name: Ricard Jaune Lemon
description: One of the rare cocktails featuring pastis as an ingredient. Orgeat syrup and lemon juice tame the anise spirit, topped with water for a refreshing, licorice-accented long drink.
ingredients:
Pastis – 20 ml
 Orgeat Syrup – 10 ml
 Lemon Juice – 20 ml
 Water – 160 ml

name: Richmond Gimlet
description: This Gimlet variation was created in 2001 by Jeffrey Morgenthaler. Gin, lime juice, and simple syrup form the classic sour base, elevated with a fresh sprig of muddled mint.
ingredients:
Gin – 60 ml
 Lime Juice – 30 ml
 Simple Syrup – 15 ml
 Mint – 1 sprig
 Mint – 1 sprig

name: Rob Roy
description: Created at the Waldorf Astoria in 1894 for the operetta Rob Roy, based on the Scottish hero. Similar to a Manhattan but made with Scotch instead of bourbon, with sweet vermouth and bitters.
ingredients:
Scotch Whiskey – 60 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 30 ml
 Peychaud's Bitters – 2 dash
 Cherry – 1
 Lemon Peel – 1

name: Rosita
description: A drink for fans of slightly bitter cocktails like the Negroni. Dating from the late 1980s, it features tequila, Campari, both sweet and dry vermouth, and a dash of Angostura bitters.
ingredients:
Tequila – 45 ml
 Campari – 30 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 15 ml
 Dry Vermouth – 15 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 1 dash
 Orange Peel – 1

name: Rusty Nail
description: A mix of Scotch whisky and Drambuie, where the sweetness of the liqueur mellows the bite of the Scotch. Cocktails similar to the Rusty Nail have been around since the late 1930s.
ingredients:
Scotch Whiskey – 60 ml
 Drambuie – 20 ml
 Lemon Peel – 1

name: Sazerac
description: A New Orleans classic created around 1850, named after a cognac brand. Originally made with absinthe, the recipe evolved to use rye whiskey and Peychaud's bitters. Louisiana's official cocktail.
ingredients:
Rye – 60 ml
 Simple Syrup – 15 ml
 Peychaud's Bitters – 3 dash

name: Scofflaw
description: First served at Harry's Bar in Paris in 1924. The term Scofflaw described those who secretly drank alcohol during Prohibition. Rye, dry vermouth, lemon juice, and grenadine.
ingredients:
Rye – 60 ml
 Dry Vermouth – 20 ml
 Lemon Juice – 15 ml
 Grenadine – 7 ml
 Orange Bitters – 1 dash

name: Screaming Orgasm
description: Described as "like drinking ice cream," this creamy cocktail is served on the rocks or as a shooter. Coffee liqueur, Irish cream, amaretto, vodka, and cream make a rich indulgence.
ingredients:
Coffee Liqueur – 15 ml
 Irish Cream – 15 ml
 Amaretto – 15 ml
 Vodka – 30 ml
 Half & Half – 90 ml
 Orange – 1 slice

name: Screwdriver
description: A very simple cocktail of vodka and orange juice. The first reference to a Screwdriver appeared in Time magazine in 1949. Legend says it was named by oil workers who stirred it with a screwdriver.
ingredients:
Vodka – 45 ml
 Orange Juice – 120 ml

name: Sea Breeze
description: Dating from the 1920s, originally made with gin and grenadine. Since cranberry juice became popular in the 1950s, the Sea Breeze has featured vodka, cranberry, and grapefruit juice.
ingredients:
Vodka – 60 ml
 Cranberry Juice – 45 ml
 Grapefruit Juice – 45 ml
 Lime – 1 wedge

name: Seelbach
description: Created at the Seelbach Hotel in Louisville, Kentucky in 1917. The recipe was lost during Prohibition but "rediscovered" in 1995. Bourbon, Cointreau, bitters, and sparkling wine.
ingredients:
Bourbon – 30 ml
 Cointreau – 15 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 7 dash
 Peychaud's Bitters – 7 dash
 Sparkling Wine – 120 ml
 Orange Peel – 1

name: Shandygaff
description: Most recipes call for equal parts ale and ginger beer. This version, adapted from a recipe by Charles Dickens' great-grandson Cedric Dickens, adds orange curaçao for extra complexity.
ingredients:
Ale – 150 ml
 Ginger Beer – 150 ml
 Lemon Juice – 15 ml
 Orange Curacao – 15 ml

name: Sidecar
description: First appeared at the end of WWI in either London or Paris, with recipes published in 1922. Similar to a Daiquiri but uses brandy instead of rum, served with a sugar-crusted rim.
ingredients:
Lime – 1 wedge
 Granulated Sugar
 Brandy – 60 ml
 Triple Sec – 30 ml
 Lemon Juice – 20 ml

name: Siesta
description: Part grapefruit margarita, part tequila daiquiri, created by New York bartender Katie Stipe. Tequila, Campari, lime, grapefruit, and simple syrup make this a citrusy, bittersweet delight.
ingredients:
Tequila Blanco – 45 ml
 Campari – 15 ml
 Simple Syrup – 15 ml
 Lime Juice – 15 ml
 Grapefruit Juice – 15 ml

name: Silver Fizz
description: From Harry Johnson's 1882 New and Improved Bartender's Manual. Old Tom gin, lemon juice, simple syrup, and egg white are shaken and topped with club soda for a classic, frothy fizz.
ingredients:
Egg white – 15 ml
 Old Tom Gin – 60 ml
 Simple Syrup – 15 ml
 Lemon Juice – 15 ml
 Club Soda – 60 ml

name: Silver Monk
description: From NYC mixologist Phil Ward, similar to a margarita but with yellow Chartreuse and muddled cucumber. Tequila, mint, and lime add layers of herbal, refreshing complexity to the blend.
ingredients:
Mint – 8 leaf
 Cucumber – 2 slice
 Salt – 1 pinch
 Simple Syrup – 15 ml
 Tequila Blanco – 60 ml
 Yellow Chartreuse – 20 ml
 Lime Juice – 30 ml
 Mint – 2 leaf
 Cucumber – 1 slice

name: Siphon Ramos Gin Fizz
description: A Ramos Gin Fizz made lazily with a siphon instead of shaking for multiple minutes. The siphon carbonates a pre-made mix, delivering the same silky, frothy result with much less effort.
ingredients:
Ramos Gin Fizz Mix – 100 ml
 Club Soda – 90 ml

name: Sloe Gin Fizz
description: A variation of the sour family with added carbonation. The term fizz first appeared in Jerry Thomas' 1887 guide. Sloe gin, lemon juice, simple syrup, and club soda create a fruity classic.
ingredients:
Sloe Gin – 45 ml
 Lemon Juice – 20 ml
 Simple Syrup – 7 ml
 Club Soda – 90 ml
 Lemon Peel – 1

name: Some Like It Hot
description: A spicy and citrusy drink created by Brandon Burkart of Haddington's in Austin, Texas. Rye, Aperol, yellow Chartreuse, orange juice, bitters, and hot sauce deliver complex heat and flavor.
ingredients:
Rye – 45 ml
 Aperol – 15 ml
 Yellow Chartreuse – 15 ml
 Orange Juice – 9 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 2 dash
 Hot Sauce – 1 dash
 Orange – 1 twist

name: Spazerack
description: An unusual frozen spin on the classic Sazerac. Rye, simple syrup, lemon juice, absinthe, and Peychaud's bitters are blended with ice for a refreshing, slushy take on the New Orleans classic.
ingredients:
Rye – 75 ml
 Simple Syrup – 15 ml
 Lemon Juice – 7 ml
 Absinthe – 2.5 ml
 Peychaud's Bitters – 3 dash
 Lemon Peel – 1

name: Spritz
description: An Italian wine-based cocktail commonly served as an aperitif in Northeast Italy. Prosecco, Aperol, and soda water combine in this light, refreshing, bittersweet drink now popular worldwide.
ingredients:
Aperol – 40 ml
 Sparkling Wine – 60 ml
 Club Soda – 20 ml

name: St. Germain Cocktail
description: A refreshingly fizzy, flowery cocktail that highlights the delicate elderflower flavors of St. Germain liqueur. Sparkling wine and club soda add effervescence to this elegant, simple drink.
ingredients:
St. Germain – 45 ml
 Sparkling Wine – 60 ml
 Club Soda – 60 ml
 Lemon Peel – 1

name: Sweet Basil #2
description: A cocktail for basil lovers from Todd Thrasher of Restaurant Eve in Alexandria, Virginia. Lillet Blanc, gin, and basil syrup combine for a fragrant, herbaceous, and refreshing drink.
ingredients:
Lillet Blanc – 90 ml
 Gin – 15 ml
 Basil Syrup – 30 ml
 Basil – 1 leaf

name: Tailspin
description: A variation of the Bijou replacing orange bitters with Campari. Equal parts gin, sweet vermouth, and green Chartreuse are enhanced with a dash of Campari for an herbal, bitter-forward cocktail.
ingredients:
Gin – 20 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 20 ml
 Green Chartreuse – 20 ml
 Campari – 2.5 ml
 Lemon – 1 twist
 Cherry – 1

name: Tampico
description: A nice long drink created by Rudolf Slavik at the George V Hotel in Paris in 1960. Triple sec, Campari, and lemon juice are topped with tonic water for a refreshingly bitter, citrusy cocktail.
ingredients:
Triple Sec – 20 ml
 Campari – 30 ml
 Lemon Juice – 20 ml
 Tonic Water – 50 ml

name: Tennessee
description: A variation on the whiskey sour using Tennessee whiskey, found in David Embury's classic 1948 The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks. Maraschino liqueur adds a subtle cherry note to the sour profile.
ingredients:
Tennessee Whiskey – 45 ml
 Lemon Juice – 20 ml
 Maraschino Liqueur – 15 ml

name: The End of the Road
description: Created by Chris McMillian and found in the excellent Beta Cocktails book. Equal parts Scotch, Campari, and green Chartreuse make a bold, herbal, and complex spirit-forward cocktail.
ingredients:
Scotch Whiskey – 30 ml
 Campari – 30 ml
 Green Chartreuse – 30 ml

name: The Espresso Martini
description: Born in the late 1980s when bartenders discovered vodka's potential with fresh ingredients. Vodka, Kahlúa, espresso, and simple syrup are shaken into a rich, caffeinated modern classic.
ingredients:
Vodka – 50 ml
 Kahlua – 15 ml
 Simple Syrup – 10 ml
 Espresso – 50 ml
 Coffee Bean – 3

name: Tipperary
description: The original Tipperary probably appeared in the early 1900s, but wasn't very tasty. This balanced version with Irish whiskey, sweet vermouth, Campari, and green Chartreuse is much better.
ingredients:
Irish Whiskey – 60 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 20 ml
 Campari – 20 ml
 Green Chartreuse – 7 ml
 Orange Peel – 1

name: Tom Collins
description: First published in 1876 by Jerry Thomas. Named after the Great Tom Collins Hoax of 1874, when pranksters would send people to bars asking for the non-existent "Tom Collins." Gin, lemon, and soda.
ingredients:
Gin – 45 ml
 Lemon Juice – 20 ml
 Simple Syrup – 15 ml
 Club Soda – 120 ml
 Lemon – 1 wedge

name: Too Close For Comfort
description: From Difford's Guide, adapted from a recipe at Mezza9 in Singapore. Vodka and Southern Comfort are mixed with lemon juice and simple syrup for a smooth, citrusy, easy-drinking cocktail.
ingredients:
Vodka – 45 ml
 Southern Comfort – 30 ml
 Lemon Juice – 30 ml
 Simple Syrup – 20 ml
 Lemon Peel – 1

name: Treacle
description: Created by Dick Bradsell, who trained many of London's top bartenders. Similar to an Old Fashioned but uses dark rum instead of bourbon, with simple syrup and Angostura bitters.
ingredients:
Dark Rum – 60 ml
 Simple Syrup – 7 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 1 dash
 Orange Peel – 1

name: Trinidad
description: A variation of Rum and Coke created by Dale DeGroff, where bitters add complexity. Light rum, a generous amount of Angostura bitters, and cola with lime create a deeper, more nuanced highball.
ingredients:
Light Rum – 45 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 4 dash
 Cola – 150 ml
 Lime – 1 wedge

name: Twelve Mile Limit
description: A Prohibition-era cocktail named after the "Rum Line," which prohibited alcohol consumption within 12 miles of American shores. Rum, rye, brandy, grenadine, and lemon juice.
ingredients:
Light Rum – 30 ml
 Rye – 15 ml
 Brandy – 15 ml
 Grenadine – 15 ml
 Lemon Juice – 15 ml
 Lemon Peel – 1

name: Vendome
description: Dating from the 1930s, this was the house cocktail at the Vendome Club in Hollywood. Equal parts gin, Dubonnet, and dry vermouth are stirred and garnished with a lemon twist for a balanced aperitif.
ingredients:
Gin – 30 ml
 Dubonnet – 30 ml
 Dry Vermouth – 30 ml
 Lemon Peel – 1 twist

name: Vesper
description: Created by James Bond in Ian Fleming's 1953 Casino Royale, named after Vesper Lynd. Gin, vodka, and Lillet Blanc are shaken together for an elegant, potent drink. Recipe credited to Ivar Bryce.
ingredients:
Gin – 60 ml
 Vodka – 30 ml
 Lillet Blanc – 15 ml
 Lemon Peel – 1

name: Vieux Carré
description: A 1930s cocktail from New Orleans, named after the city's famed French Quarter. Cognac, rye, sweet vermouth, Benedictine, and both Angostura and Peychaud's bitters make a rich, complex classic.
ingredients:
Cognac – 30 ml
 Rye – 30 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 30 ml
 Benedictine – 5.0 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 2 dash
 Peychaud's Bitters – 2 dash
 Cherry – 1

name: Vodka Gimlet
description: A variation of the classic Gimlet using vodka instead of gin. The neutral spirit lets the tartness of lime juice shine, balanced with simple syrup for a clean, refreshing citrus cocktail.
ingredients:
Vodka – 60 ml
 Lime Juice – 30 ml
 Simple Syrup – 20 ml
 Lime – 1 wedge

name: Vodka Martini
description: Also known as a Vodkatini or Kangaroo Cocktail. Purists insist martinis must contain gin, but this iconic vodka version can be stirred or shaken à la James Bond, with olive or lemon twist.
ingredients:
Vodka – 75 ml
 Dry Vermouth – 20 ml
 Lemon Peel – 1

name: Ward 8
description: Invented in Boston in 1898, this cocktail is a cousin of the Whiskey Sour. Rye, lemon juice, orange juice, and grenadine are shaken and topped with a splash of soda for a fruity, balanced drink.
ingredients:
Rye – 60 ml
 Lemon Juice – 15 ml
 Orange Juice – 15 ml
 Grenadine – 7 ml
 Club Soda – 15 ml

name: Whiskey Collins
description: A Tom Collins variation using bourbon instead of gin. Bourbon, lemon juice, and simple syrup are shaken, poured over ice, and topped with club soda for a refreshing, whiskey-forward cooler.
ingredients:
Bourbon – 45 ml
 Lemon Juice – 20 ml
 Simple Syrup – 15 ml
 Club Soda – 120 ml
 Lemon – 1 wedge

name: Whiskey Sour
description: First referred to in print in a Wisconsin newspaper in 1870. The sour family features a base spirit, citrus juice, and a sweetener. Bourbon, lemon juice, and simple syrup define this classic.
ingredients:
Bourbon – 60 ml
 Lemon Juice – 20 ml
 Simple Syrup – 15 ml
 Cherry – 1

name: White Lady
description: Also known as a Delilah or Chelsea Sidecar, this is a Sidecar made with gin instead of brandy. Gin, triple sec, lemon juice, and egg white are shaken to produce a frothy, elegant classic.
ingredients:
Egg white – 15 ml
 Lemon Juice – 20 ml
 Gin – 45 ml
 Triple Sec – 20 ml
 Lemon Peel – 1

name: White Negroni
description: A lighter, more delicate take on the classic Negroni. Gin is paired with Suze gentian liqueur and Lillet Blanc instead of Campari and sweet vermouth, creating a floral, bittersweet aperitif.
ingredients:
Gin – 30 ml
 Suze – 30 ml
 Lillet Blanc – 30 ml
 Orange Peel – 1

name: White Negroni #2 - Less Suze
description: A softer variation on the White Negroni with less Suze and more Lillet Blanc. This shifts the balance toward floral and wine-like notes while retaining the gentian's subtle bitterness.
ingredients:
Gin – 30 ml
 Suze – 15 ml
 Lillet Blanc – 45 ml

name: White Russian
description: A variation of the 1949 Black Russian, adding cream. Made famous as the favorite drink of The Dude in the 1998 Coen Brothers film The Big Lebowski, who called it a "Caucasian."
ingredients:
Vodka – 30 ml
 Coffee Liqueur – 30 ml
 Cream – 30 ml

name: WP Martinez
description: A tailor-made cocktail recipe created and shared by a fellow home mixologist. White Penja pepper syrup adds an exotic spicy note to this gin-based Martinez variation with sweet vermouth.
ingredients:
Paragon White Penja Pepper – 15 ml
 Gin – 45 ml
 Sweet Vermouth – 20 ml
 Maraschino Liqueur – 5 ml
 Angostura Bitters – 2 drop

name: Yellow Bird
description: An exotic Caribbean cocktail. This sophisticated version omits the extra fruit juices of other recipes, letting light rum, lime juice, triple sec, and Galliano shine in a clean, tropical blend.
ingredients:
Light Rum – 45 ml
 Lime Juice – 15 ml
 Triple Sec – 15 ml
 Galliano – 15 ml
 Lime – 1 wedge
